sentences.text: Right.
So I got to Buchenwald, and I start to look for my father.
And somebody had told me that the Hungarian block is in a certain spot.
And I went there, and they said, how're you going to find your father, you know?
There is no records or anything like that.
And I figured I'd stay.
So what happened-- Buchenwald had 20,000 men, and I was the third woman to get into Buchenwald to look for, for relative.
So they told me there is two girls in that room.
I should go and room with them.
And then the next day, you will see what you could do.
But they didn't give me too much of hope.
There was a Hungarian man.
But I, I don't know who it was.
But he was from Buchenwald.
And I told him from what city I came from and how old my father was.
So he says to me, it's the oldest person probably over here it's in [INAUDIBLE].
So, you know, he didn't give me too much of anything.
But I don't want to give up when I stayed there.
And that's how I met my husband.
Like-- Were there other people-- you said there were just two other girls.
Two other girls.
They were also coming in to look for their relative, and they got there a day before me.
So they weren't there all that long.
So they didn't know too much.
And actually, one of them was from Romania.
And she didn't speak a very good Hungarian.
But we got along, and I stayed there.
And I looked and looked.
But I did find people from not too far from our town, and everybody said that would be impossible to find anybody.
And I should look around and see what it is, because the bodies were still there.
They were still bringing in the Americans to see actually what really happened there.
The Americans brought in the-- from different places, the soldiers to look around.
There was no Germans around.
I couldn't see any Germans.
It was mostly all American.
I was sick.
I, I was sick.
Everybody was shot dead.
At least in my camp, we were working.
We didn't see that kind of things, you know.
We didn't have no crematory, and we didn't have gas chambers.
We had to work very hard, and we worked with poison.
Like, they don't want to use the Germans for that, so they used us for that.
But we weren't beaten every day, you know, like-- or hanged.
Over here, people were hanging.
And people were laying on the ground turned over, one with the stomach, one with the other.
And there were bones.
And it was a terrible scene, really.
You had to be--
In Buchenwald, well, I, I stayed there till the end, until they liquidated the whole camp, because the Russians were going to take over that part.
So we went in deeper to Germany.
My husband.
At that point, I met him already.
And--
--anybody who didn't want to stay on the Russian side went away.
Well, the way I met my husband in Buchenwald is that we were three girls in the room.
And that one day I had to clean up the room, because it was my turn.
We made up that every day, somebody else going to.
So I cleaned up the room.
And all of a sudden, somebody comes and want to do disinfection, because they were full of lice and all kind of problems.
So my husband already was working with them to disinfect the people not to have lice in them.
And he was going to come in, spill things all over the room, and I just cleaned it.
So I couldn't speak to him, so I said, I, I just cleaned.
I show him the door to go out.
Meanwhile I was scared, because I, I didn't understand him, and he didn't understood me too well.
And one thing I did understand that he's going to come back around 3 o'clock.
And I figured he's going to come back at 3 o'clock to punish me.
Or I really thought he was a German, the way he was dressed.
So I tried to run away from the block.
And when you see so many people and everybody so skinny and everybody wearing the same clothes, I didn't really recognize him anymore between them.
But he did, because there was only three women there.
And he was trying to talk to me.
And I was very scared, and I started to run the other way.
The block was like-- they had a door in the front where you walk in, and on the other side, you could run out.
So when I saw him coming towards, into the thing, I took off.
And I went out on the other side.
But he had two other friends, and they caught up with me.
Matter of fact, one of them is the one I told you that he's in New York.
So I started to run away, and somehow they always tried to talk to me.
And, and I don't know how we really get together.
It's, it's a history.
It's a mystery.
[LAUGHS]
No, because he talked to me German, and I was afraid that he's a German.
And I do not-- I did not understand the language.
And I was Hungarian, so I couldn't talk to him.
And Jewish I didn't understand.
He spoke a few languages, but I didn't, so I couldn't talk to him at all.
I, I stayed in the same place in Buchenwald, in this, this same room with the two girls, until they said that it's going to be liquidated.
And then we just have to go to further into Germany.
And I was willing to do that.
But in between, I went back to Hamburg somehow.
I had some belonging there.
I brought it back with me to Buchenwald.
In that point already was more-- I was able to go a little better around, when I know where I was or somehow.
Scared.
[LAUGHS] Scared all the time.
Very scared, because you, you didn't know who you could talk to, who you can't.
And as I said, I had language problem all the time.
So it was very hard.
But in Buchenwald, before we would have gone away from there, they had a room where they took away the belonging from the Jews.
And somehow they couldn't take it with them.
So the Hungarian Jews got together, because they felt they were the last ones, and they were probably belonging to them.
So they started to make little packages for each and every one of us who they knew that were around and Hungarian.
And there was gold there.
It was silver there.
And it was diamonds there.
So you have to be smart to pick whatever you really wanted.
Around the corner, I saw a sewing machine.
And I just wanted the sewing machine.
I didn't care about anything else.
So I picked up that sewing machine.
And I started to make some shirts from sheets for my husband and for the other friends.
And I made a skirt.
No.
We didn't even-- no.
And I, I made a skirt for myself and a blouse, so I could change clothes.
And I was very happy with this stupid sewing machine over there.
And I started to work right away to do something.
Yeah, they took us also with a truck to the station.
And at the station we, we went by train.
And then we were supposed to go from there to Bamberg, to more like a civilian place to be, and not in the camp now.
But somehow, all guys who are hungry, but I don't know what they did wrong.
We was staying in a-- Abe going to tell you the rest of it-- in a place where the trains were.
And they saw the American train was coming in with food.
They had conserves on it.
They had peaches and pears in, in big can.
And they started to steal some, because they were hungry.
So we got punished.
We were sent to another camp.
From there we went to [NON-ENGLISH].
And we stayed there for a while, until we got transported into Landsberg.
It was a whole big thing, but he remembers more dates and everything than I do.
Right.
This was a displaced person camp.
When we were there already we, we got used to, to our troubles, and we know we have no place to go.
We just have to do whatever we could do for ourselves.
So that was-- first I started to make a kosher kitchen where we could eat.
And there was many other people who wanted to eat kosher.
I think they had about 3,000 people there, but out of the 3,000, maybe 100, 150 who kept-- who eat kosher.
So I was working making challah and-- for the first Rosh Hashanah, I, I remember making challah and cholent.
And I wanted to make some very good cookies.
And they brought us some margarine, so I made it so good that everything went flat [LAUGHS] from the margarine.
I put in too much, and we just could eat the crumbs.
There was nothing to eat.
And then later the-- Who ran the camp?
Where did the food come from?
UNRRA, UNRRA.
That was from Americans already.
They run everything.
And then we started to get clothes.
And then we started to get food.
And we also get cigarette, what we didn't use.
So out of the cigarette, we could buy for ourself things, because we sold it.
And we started to get to be a little bit more on the normal side of it.
Well, because before we were like not human, you know.
We, we didn't know what's happening to us.
We were just thrown here.
We had to do whatever they told us to do.
Here, already, we didn't have to be so afraid.
We were only between Jews.
And we were watching out for each other.
Well, in the beginning, wasn't.
But then they separated.
They took out all the non-Jews, and Landsberg become a Jewish camp.
I stayed there for three years.
We were there for-- because I got married in 1946.
And we came to America 1949.
It, it was-- it wasn't easy.
We have to work.
But you know, we, we know we have no home.
We, we know we have no parents.
We know we don't have anything.
So we just have to make the best whatever we could, and, and friends become families.
You know, like I had a big wedding.
There 100 people, but not even one person from my family was there.
It was just-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's the way it was.
And my husband tried to make a, a, a living.
He wasn't my husband yet, but he started to work that-- you know--
We, we saved--
I started to-- first I went to [INAUDIBLE], and I was trying to teach sewing, a little bit.
And then they opened a [NON-ENGLISH] because Israel become a state in 1948.
And they needed people to make army clothes for the American army.
So I volunteered.
And I--
I mean for in the Israeli army.
So I volunteered, and we were making shirts for them.
So I was working in the shirt factory for-- until I came out to America.
I went back to Hungary before I got married.
I-- when we got to Landsberg, and we were stationed there, I just want to go home to make sure that there is no survivors there, even though I know it.
But somehow, I just had a feeling to go home and make sure.
So I went home and looked for my family.
But I couldn't find nobody.
Wasn't easy, because I had no money.
So-- and somehow at that time, you could take the train.
You don't have to have the money.
So I was taking a few trains.
Finally, I got to Vienna.
And Vienna, all you had to really do is walk over the border and you were in Hungary.
And that's really what, what happened, because the trains weren't going.
So there were a whole bunch of people.
It wasn't myself.
Then they had the soup kitchens in between in different cities that they were looking for people like we were.
And they were feeding us.
And there are-- there were the one who told me, well, if you go here, you could go that far.
If you go there, you, you know-- nobody really know.
Right.
They were all--
--refugees, and nobody really knows what really was.
Not at all.
I, I, I couldn't speak to them, so I couldn't really, you know, relate with them at all.
I was just-- did meet the refugees themselves.
But it was plenty around in everywhere.
And true-- at night, they, they didn't really let you walk through the border, but there was a couple of them who know how to go back and forth.
And they were taking us back and forth to go there.
So then finally I got to Hungary.
And there, too, the trains weren't moving.
And I didn't have no money.
So I went also to another soup kitchen where they-- all the refugees were gathering.
And they told me to wait for a certain time, and a horse and buggy comes from one town to the other town, and you could go with them.
I went with them, and then I had a very bad experience there, because I had a loose thing in my hand.
It was hardly anything that I took with me, but they give me conserve, what the Americans were getting from them to eat-- what did they call that?
The little conserve food.
There was cheese in that, and all kinds-- K-rations?
That's what it was.
That's what I had.
But they wanted to take it away from me.
This Hungarians who take you from one place to the other.
And as we were going towards my own hometown, we weren't too far away from them, like walking distance.
They decided to put like a [NON-ENGLISH] on my hand.
See that little mark over here?
It's a very fine white line there, but this is right on the, the blood's--
Vein goes through.
And, and as they put this on, this was a blood sucker.
So it sucked my blood out.
And I fainted.
And they put me down on the ground.
Between one city to the other it was only fields.
And I must have been there for hours.
But God knows how long, because I had no watch or anything.
And then I wake up, and I had nothing, nothing at all.
I had a, a little shawl on me.
They took that, too.
But I know where I was.
I just walk home to my hometown, and hopefully I find some people there, and I'll have some food.
Which I did, because one of the lady was home already who I sent home, because I find out when I was going to Buchenwald that her husband was there and her son.
And they went home already.
So I went back to her to tell her, that time, that your son and your husband is home, you should go home.
So she appreciated it so much that when I came home, she didn't know what to do with me.
And she fed me, and she got me another [NON-ENGLISH] to wear.
And she said stay with us.
Don't go back.
You know, we'll find room for you.
Because they were older and they were my parents' friends.
But after that incident, I just couldn't stand anything.
And when I wanted to go into the house, I just couldn't get into the house.
I started to cry--
Right.
I started to cry, and nothing helped.
She came with me.
And I, I just pulled her back to go back to her house.
And then finally I decided this is not a way to be.
And they told me that I have all my first cousins in Papa, so I went there.
That was another town.
But she helped me to-- with somebody to go there.
So I wasn't on my own already.
They helped me to go there.
And they came with me.
And then they decided, also, that it's no life home.
They're going to go back to Germany.
So one of my cousin did come back to Germany with me.
And the others didn't feel like giving up their place.
Like, one of them was a baker, and he had the bakery, so he felt he has to make a living for himself at home.
He was older than me, and he wasn't going to come.
They probably took everything away, whatever they could.
But I don't know.
I couldn't even look through the window.
I was so stupid.
After, when I went back to Germany, I realized I could have found a picture from the family or something.
But you know, when, when you are so upset-- and it was just very hard to get used to it.
And everybody who was--
I, I was probably 18, a little over 18.
I was-- definitely wasn't 19.
So I was 18.
And I just couldn't wait to leave the town, because everybody faces that I had seen when we were going away, they were so happy that we're leaving, that I just couldn't live with them.
And I didn't find anybody.
I did go out to the cemetery to my grandma's grave site, and that was the end of that.
They, they tried, they tried to be friendly, but the incident that they did to me, I didn't trust them no more, you know.
I lost faith in everybody.
I, I just thought that-- and also, that one of them who lived next to our house, and they said, well, you don't have anything left.
Whatever they left here, your parents, well, they took it away.
Meanwhile, over there, you had to go up to the attic from the outside of the house.
You're going into the attic, and I-- when they open the attic, I could see there was some furniture of ours.
So I lost all the faith in everybody.
And I just couldn't stay.
It was a small place.
It was like everybody knew everybody.
And I just lost the feeling towards every one of them.
Even the, the mayor was so good to my father.
And when he saw me, he told me that he has a few things for me, which he give back to me.
And he said, one thing you must do.
You must take your birth certificate with you, whatever you go.
That way you need eventually.
So I, I got that.
But they weren't good to him, either, because he was good to the Jews.
They took away his children to Auschwitz also.
He didn't have his children, and they throw him out of his job, because eventually they find out that he was helping Jews.
So he got punished in a way, too.
But I didn't want it to create more trouble for him, so I didn't want he should have to bother with me that he had more hard time down there.
And I just left.
When you went back to Germany, to Landsberg, what were you hoping would happen next?
In that point, I know I have to start a life on my own, because there's really nobody for me to be.
And I don't want hang around.
So we got married, because I thought he was a better way to belong to somebody than to just to be by myself.
And I didn't want to do anything without my parents knowing it, that I'm getting married, because I know him before already from Buchenwald.
Well, I just felt I, I need approval, but I didn't get it, because there was nobody around.
And that was it.
In Landsberg, in 1946.
But we really didn't had a life until we came to America, because--
Right, because that American soldiers did find my family.
They, they find from my father's side here the [NON-ENGLISH] in Bridgeport.
I don't know if you heard of them.
And from my mother's side-- my mother was a [NON-ENGLISH] girl, so they find from London, the [NON-ENGLISH].
And matter of fact, in Germany, they sent somebody, one of the-- he was in the Army.
He was a rabbi for America.
And they contact him to come and visit me in Landsberg.
And he came, and he give me $20.
And $20 was, like, a lot of money to us that somebody should give you, because we didn't have no money.
And they want to send me anything I wanted.
I still have a letter from them that they wrote.
The thing was that I couldn't go to England, because England in those days didn't let the Jews in.
But they had somebody on Fifth Avenue in America, and they sent us the address that I should write to them.
And they'll do anything that I want to do.
And I could get an affidavit by, by them, from my father's side.
I got the affidavit from Bridgeport, so I don't want it to mix things up.
And so we got to Bridgeport.
'49, In April.
In America?
Well, it was wonderful, because this was my father's first cousins.
They weren't that close relative, but I called them uncles.
And they tried to help us every way they can.
But those days, in America, wasn't easy to get a job, either, because everybody came home from the war and, you know, things weren't easy.
So one of them said, don't worry.
You're going to work enough yet.
Take it easy a little bit.
And it's, it's not a problem.
They'll feed us, and they rented an apartment for us.
And they wanted to give me money to buy furniture.
And whenever we'll have money, we'll give it back.
And my husband said no.
We're going to live in a furnished apartment, and when we could afford it, we're going to buy our own.
And actually, this is what he-- happened.
We, we started to work after a few weeks in a cleaning place.
And, and we were getting-- I was getting $0.65.
He was getting $0.75.
And one of my aunts said, she said, you're too good.
You know how to sew.
I'm going to take you to another place, and maybe they'll give you a job.
So she took me to a coat factory, where they really gave me a job.
And I did a lot of good for them.
And I was very happy.
I worked like, like a horse in the end.
I started to work in the morning from 8:30, and I worked to 5 o'clock in the afternoon.
And then I learned this new trade, how to make the hems on the coats and the facing on the coats.
And I become very fast.
I was a very fast worker.
And then I got into trouble with the union, because I knocked down the work for the other people.
On the coat factory?
I, I worked on this coat factory over a year.
Yes.
Then I went to another factory, because this one was sold.
And I went to the next factory, and that's where I became pregnant.
And I-- this was in 1952.
In August.
Should I talk, or stop?
